file_name: Monsanto Company - SECOND A_R EXCLUSIVE AGENCY AND MARKETING AGREEMENT .txt

perturbation.original_text: The Agent and Monsanto shall each maintain true and complete records in connection with this Agreement and shall retain all such records for at least forty-eight (48) months following the termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agent and Monsanto shall each maintain true and complete records in connection with this Agreement and shall retain all such records for at least twelve (12) months following the termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the records retention period to 12 months introduces a potential legal contradiction. The change reduces the amount of time that records are held for.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act mandates specific record retention policies for publicly traded companies and their subsidiaries. While this agreement doesn't explicitly state that either company is publicly traded, Monsanto is a Delaware corporation and potentially subject to SOX. A 12-month retention period may be insufficient to comply with SOX requirements for retaining audit work papers and other relevant documents, which typically require a longer retention period (e.g., 5-7 years) to ensure accurate financial reporting and potential audits. Thus, a 12-month record retention conflicts with the compliance expectations of SOX.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the record retention period from 48 months to 12 months. The Law Explanation states that this shorter period may be insufficient to comply with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) requirements for audit work papers, which typically require a retention period of 5-7 years. The explanation suggests that the shorter period conflicts with SOX compliance expectations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'PUBLIC COMPANY ACCOUNTING REFORM AND CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY', which is the title of SOX (15 USC Ch. 98). Therefore, because the reduced time period potentially violates SOX, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed given on the same business day if delivered personally or sent by telefax with confirmation of receipt, on the next business day if sent by overnight courier, or on the earlier of actual receipt as shown on the registered receipt or five business days after mailing if mailed by registered or certified mail (return receipt requested) to the parties at the addresses set forth below (or at such other address for a party as shall be specified by like notice):
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed given immediately if delivered personally, one week after sent by telefax without confirmation of receipt, never if sent by overnight courier, or one month after mailing if mailed by standard mail (return receipt not requested) to the parties at the addresses set forth below (or at such other address for a party as shall be specified by like notice):
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notice provisions to make them less reliable and slower introduces legal contradiction. Specifically, saying notice is deemed to occur one week after sending by fax *without* confirmation or one month after sending by mail *without* return receipt makes it more difficult to prove notice occurred.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles of reasonable notice
perturbation.law_explanation: Standard contract law, as reflected in the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) and general common law principles, requires that notice be 'reasonable' under the circumstances. Changing the notification structure of this contract conflicts with requirements for fair and reasonable communication. The changes create ambiguity and difficulty in demonstrating effective notice has been received.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has " notice " of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) " Knowledge " means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) " Discover ", " learn ", or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person " notifies " or " gives " a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person " receives " a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties or the individual has reason to k
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it makes the notification process unreliable, conflicting with the requirement for 'reasonable' notice as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 1-202 (d), states a person 'notifies' or 'gives' a notice...by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it.' The changed text specifies methods without confirmation of receipt, such as sending a fax or mail without return receipt requested, which makes it difficult to prove notice was received. This contradicts the reasonable steps requirement because it makes it harder to verify actual notification and deviates from standard business practices where receipt confirmation is usually required for important notices. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition confirms that this change, which alters time-sensitive obligations, violates legal standards. Therefore, the modification introduces terms that undermine the reliability of the notification process, leading to a direct contradiction of the law.
